title: Cat Falling Over to One Side: Emergency Guide and Causes
meta_desc: Is your cat falling to one side? This High Urgency symptom requires immediate vet care. Learn about vestibular disease and how to help your pet.
content_html:
What is Ataxia and Vestibular Dysfunction?

When a cat repeatedly falls to one side, it is typically experiencing a loss of balance known as ataxia or vestibular dysfunction. The vestibular system, located in the inner ear and brain, acts like a carpenter's level to keep the animal upright. When this system is disrupted, the cat may feel intense dizziness, causing them to lean, fall, or circle in one direction.

Urgency Level: High

A cat suddenly losing its balance is considered a veterinary emergency. This symptom can be caused by various serious conditions, including inner ear infections, neurological inflammatory diseases, strokes, or exposure to toxins. Because these conditions can progress rapidly, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for an evaluation.

Why a Video or Photo Helps Triage

Taking a quick video of your cat's behavior can be an invaluable tool for the veterinary team. Cats often hide their symptoms or behave differently due to the stress of being at a clinic. A video allows the vet to see the exact nature of the falling, the direction of any head tilt, and specific eye movements (nystagmus) that help them pinpoint whether the issue is in the ear or the brain.

faqs.question: What are common signs that accompany falling over?
faqs.answer: You may also notice a persistent head tilt, eyes darting back and forth rapidly (nystagmus), nausea, or crying out in distress.

faqs.question: Could my cat have had a stroke?
faqs.answer: While strokes are less common in cats than humans, they can occur and cause sudden balance loss. Only a veterinarian can differentiate a stroke from other vestibular issues through diagnostic imaging.

faqs.question: Should I try to make my cat walk to see if they are better?
faqs.answer: No. Keep your cat in a confined, padded area like a carrier or a room with rugs to prevent them from injuring themselves while falling until you can reach a vet.
